title: Not worth deteriorating your mental health for management
pros: friendships among coworkers; birthday PTO (because who wants to deal with this BS on their birthday)
cons: Where do I even start? It's all about the money and reaching quotas with these people. All lead care managers I've worked with really care for their clients, but middle management especially at the Cerritos location, couldn't care less about these members. Management always seemed too busy or inconvenienced whenever we asked questions. Any concerns shared with our manager/supervisor were deemed as "complaints" and us giving "pushback". We were often gaslit and manipulated. All our personal achievements were used as success stories by middle management to make them look good. Training is a joke--training consists of sitting in webinars; you don't really learn anything useful. There are very few resources to provide to members in need. It's a requirement to meet members in person every so often (which was fine), but our office is primarily women (except for the supervisor), so the thought of commuting to meet a stranger in person, alone, with no real safety procedures and training set in stone was a major concern when it was first implemented and even is now. When most of us met some of these members and were faced with uncomfortable experiences and inappropriate behavior with a few bad seeds, these concerns were voiced to the manager, but we were gaslit and told "oh this doesn't happen often". Thanks for the support. I was told I'd be getting a raise during my first quarterly review. I never saw that come to fruition (been there a year or so). I received a smaller holiday bonus than those whose work quality was way below the standard. I worked hard in this company because I cared for my members, but my successes and achievements didn't amount to anything than to be used as success stories to make the boss man look good (but my documentation template, which I was praised for, was shared with everyone in the office because it was so great -- still no raise though). Turn over rate is ridiculous. They don't promote from within (at least not at Cerritos), they'd rather hire incapable outsiders for managerial roles rather than provide current employees with better opportunities
advice: You need better training, better benefits for the BS you put your employees through, and Jesus. Seriously

title: A splash of reality
pros: I've been fortunate to work in various environments, yet none have compared to the positive atmosphere and exceptional leadership here. Despite the challenges we encounter, this workplace, under our manager's guidance, feels like a second family. Our manager, who genuinely values each team member, fosters a culture of recognition and support for our achievements. She openly acknowledges that perfection isn't the goal, but prioritizes our well-being above all else, a sentiment echoed in our daily meetings. It's disheartening to read negative comments about our management, as I've experienced firsthand their kindness and human approach. Our manager's initiatives, from morning meetings to team events and birthday celebrations, reflect a commitment to creating an inclusive, respectful, and nurturing workplace. Their dedication to equality and a healthy environment is commendable, coupled with their wisdom, quick thinking, and personal support for both members and employees. Admittedly, this job isn't easy; it demands teamwork, resilience, and patience. However, the joy of helping others and the strength gained through overcoming challenges make it uniquely fulfilling. Those who solely seek a paycheck or lack the willingness to embrace challenges may struggle here, as this incredible team thrives on dedication and hard work
cons: There are no cons to be shared
advice: Keep up the amazing work, I support the team and company you all have built here 🫶

title: Let’s talk about our SD site manager
pros: I am thoroughly impressed by the exceptional environment and the quality of individuals at Serene. Our site manager in San Diego adeptly oversees a team of over 50 employees, demonstrating not only flawless management skills but also a genuine concern for the well-being of her team. Her dedication to the welfare of others is truly commendable, often going the extra mile not just for her staff but for their individual members as well. I must take a moment to commend our site manager; rarely have I encountered a superior who is so attentive and willing to work together to find solutions for any challenges faced. She embodies a blend of problem-solving abilities, compassion, and an innate drive for achieving goals, making her a natural and exemplary leader. The sense of familial belonging that Serene cultivates from day one is remarkable; the warmth extended to me upon joining was truly remarkable. It's disheartening to hear negative remarks about our site manager because, in my experience, I couldn't have wished for a better job. It's not just about the opportunity to enhance people's lives daily, but the genuine and unwavering support from our site manager that surpasses any expectations one could have
cons: I have no negative feedback

title: Only if you need experience and/or a job for now
body: My typical day started with anxiety. This company definitely cares more about numbers over quality of care. I was anxious to meet my call quota. I had to have faith and courage to focus on giving quality help with my members and risk not meeting the quota They are big on micromanaging which includes watching you with the cameras. I was constantly berated for not meeting quota even though I repeated requested non active members be removed from my caseload. Also they keep non active members on caseloads to boost their numbers. There are good team leads but they can only do so much because the supervisors are task masters that smile in your face but help with nothing. This place is polluted with favoritism. Get your experience and move on when you get it. Extremely high turnover rate.

cons: Micromanaging, belittling, and favoritism

body: Overall, a great place to work prioritizes the well-being and growth of its employees, cultivates a positive and inclusive culture, and provides opportunities for meaningful work and advancement. A workplace with a positive culture fosters collaboration, open communication, and mutual respect among colleagues. Employees feel valued, supported, and motivated to perform their best. Effective leadership sets the tone for the entire organization. Leaders who are transparent, empathetic, and inspirational can empower employees, provide clear direction, and promote a sense of trust and confidence in the company's vision and values.

I hope to see positive changes in the future, but as of now, it’s not an environment I would recommend.

title: Most toxic environment ever!
body: Hands down, the very worst company I've ever worked for. The lack of effective leadership is evident because they do NOT support the employees. Whenever you ask for support with a member, they ask you to email but blatantly disregard their emails so you are left unsupported. The upper leadership makes sure to push out the staff that are effective and genuinely care about their work performance and their members. The only thing they are concerned about is making your calls and do not focus on "quality" when it comes to helping the homeless population find housing. It's all about making sure that they can bill the various Cal-Aim programs for money. The environment is extremely fake and so is the authenticity of the leadership. If you are looking for a job where you will not be appreciated and you get constantly brushed off and lied to, this is definitely the job for you! If you are passionate about helping the homeless population find housing and you genuinely care about what you are doing, definitely don't go to this employer. They will burn you out and sour your taste for working with a disadvantaged group of people.

title: RUN dont walk away Associate Marriage and Family
body: What I believe I learned: I learned how to survive in chaos and how not to treat employees. I learned that burnout is real and that protecting your boundaries and mental health is essential. Unfortunately, I did not grow as a clinician here—I spent more time surviving than developing. Management: Disorganized and reactive. Policies shift constantly, often without being communicated. Feedback feels more like blame than support, and there is little investment in your development as a professional. Questions or concerns are often brushed off, and decisions are made from the top down with little transparency. Workplace culture: Toxic. The people who last here either disengage emotionally or stop asking questions. Passion and drive are not rewarded—they’re drained. If you want to clock in, do the bare minimum, and collect a paycheck, you’ll probably be fine. But if you're hoping to learn, grow, and feel supported, this is not the place.
pros: The clients. They’re the reason I stayed as long as I did. Working with them was meaningful, but they deserved clinicians who weren’t barely staying afloat
cons: Feeling like no one cares if you're okay. There’s no room to process, reflect, or even breathe. The emotional toll adds up quickly, and support is rarely offered unless you're already in crisis

title: The Office Has Fleas as of August 2025
body: A typical day at work: Answer calls, complete care plans, and follow up with members, all while navigating unclear instructions, last-minute changes, and communication breakdowns between supervisors, management, and the staffing agency. Some days included working in unsafe conditions (e.g., flea infestation) with no accommodations. What you believe you learned: How to stay adaptable and keep performing despite poor communication, lack of support, and unequal treatment. I learned to advocate for myself and others, even in an environment that discourages open feedback. Management: Overloaded supervisors due to poor scheduling. Some managers were condescending and dismissive, prioritizing metrics over employee well-being. The COO often undermined supervisors instead of providing clear direction. Workplace culture: A culture of inequality between direct hires and CSL employees, with empty claims of “everyone is equal.” Little genuine concern for staff health or morale. Communication issues were recognized internally but never addressed effectively. The hardest part of the job: Working under inconsistent and unclear leadership while being excluded from benefits and incentives that direct hires received, and dealing with unsafe work conditions. The most enjoyable part of the job: Helping members and knowing the work had a real impact on the community, even if leadership’s priorities didn’t reflect that mission.
pros: Staff are very nice, The people we assist are great
cons: Management constantly pressuring LCMs on their metrics, Ignoring the community aspect of our job

title: Overall good job but management ruins it
body: The job itself is great, you’re always occupied, time goes by quickly, and the work is very rewarding. I genuinely love what I do. Unfortunately, management makes the experience difficult. They micromanage to an extreme level, even when you’re doing excellent work and staying on top of things, which feels unnecessary. At times, they will get you in trouble over the smallest issues, which is demotivating and, I believe, one of the main reasons people quit. You might really enjoy the job in your first week, but after a while, you start to realize how poor some of the management can be. It’s not every one of them, but a few of them have ruined the experience for me, which is disappointing because I used to love working here. Co-workers are the best part of the job though.

cons: Management

title: Soul Sucking
pros: The team was cool, we trauma bonded I guess
cons: Everything. This place will bleed you dry. No growth opportunities. EXTREME micromanaging. Bad office politics. Bad benefits. Mid pay. Bad upper management. Bad PTO. Culture of threats and punishment, not celebration and reward. The punishment system is kind of like kindergarten, warnings, write ups, corrective actions. When I was at this job I was stuck completing processes that don't actually help people long term, getting chewed out every step of the way, having to beg for time off after being completely burnt out by the company culture. You will be watched and monitored on when you come in, when you leave, when you take breaks, who you talk to, how long you talk to them for, who are you friends with. When you're there, they are going to try to convince you that you are treated well and that all companies are like this. You are being GASLIT. If an employer has to tell you everyday that you are the problem, that you just need to be positive and grateful and complain less, then it should be a huge red flag. I know now that I was being gaslit because I left. No one stays for longer than a few years here. Most leave after just months. I left and will never ever ever look back, I'm so much happier

title: Great patient relationships, challenging management environment
body: A typical day as a Lead Care Manager included calling patients, documenting encounters, coordinating resources, and conducting in-person meetings. The role was very hands-on and relationship-based, which I truly enjoyed. Building trust with patients and supporting them through personal health challenges was the most rewarding part of the job. Coming from a healthcare background, this position helped me grow in communication, care coordination, and patient advocacy skills. This job is a good opportunity for gaining experience, especially for those newer to care management. However, the work environment can be challenging. There is a strong emphasis on metrics and productivity, and management closely monitors performance. While accountability is important, the approach can sometimes feel overly critical. Communication from upper leadership did not always feel supportive, particularly when team members had questions or were behind on goals. The hardest part of the job was balancing meaningful patient care with strict performance expectations. The most enjoyable part was the relationships built with patients and seeing their progress over time. Overall, while I value the experience I gained, I would recommend this company primarily to those seeking short-term experience rather than long-term growth.
pros: Meaningful patient relationships, Hands-on care coordination experience, Strong communication skill development, Exposure to resource management, Good entry-level healthcare experience
cons: Heavy emphasis on metrics with closely monitored performance, and management communication could feel overly critical rather than supportive

It is a dynamic environment where teamwork, open communication, and data-driven decision-making are truly valued. The culture encourages accountability, mutual support, and professional growth. I would definitely recommend this workplace to anyone looking to grow and make a difference.

title: Micromanaging
body: Unfortunately, the work environment at this location is extremely difficult due to poor management practices and excessive micromanagement. Employees are required to track and document nearly every task through multiple spreadsheets, outreach logs, and internal lists that are often incomplete or outdated. Instead of supporting staff, this level of micromanagement creates unnecessary stress and inefficiency. Our office is required to complete significantly more administrative tracking and outreach requirements than other locations, including additional contact attempts, swap lists, spreadsheets for in-person visits, and documentation systems that are not consistently used across the organization. This creates an unequal workload compared to other offices. Policies are also enforced inconsistently. Some employees have been disciplined for dress code violations while others have openly violated the same policies for extended periods of time without any consequences. This creates a perception of favoritism and unequal enforcement of company rules. Communication with management and HR has also been problematic. Concerns raised by employees regarding workplace issues and policy inconsistencies have gone unanswered for long periods of time. There have also been concerns related to workplace health and safety practices that were not handled appropriately, which resulted in staff becoming ill and missing work. The high turnover of employees and supervisors reflects the overall management culture and the level of frustration experienced by staff. I hope leadership takes steps to address these issues by improving communication, ensuring fair policy enforcement, and reducing unnecessary micromanagement so employees can focus on providing quality work.
